topic: A neighbor starts a renovation project
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rhythmic thudding and the smell of sawdust had become the unwelcome soundtrack to my mornings. From my kitchen window, I could watch Mr. Henderson, my usually stoic neighbor, wrestling with a suspiciously large piece of lumber right outside his property line. He was wearing a bright orange safety vest, which seemed to amplify the sheer scale of his undertaking. Every time he paused to wipe sweat from his brow, he’d glance over, and I would instinctively shrink back, pulling the curtains tighter around my windows as if I could somehow become invisible. I tried to focus on my coffee, but the sheer volume of the demolition work made my stomach clench. I kept catching my reflection in the glass—a pale, slightly flushed face, my shoulders hunched inward, a desperate urge to retreat into the shadows of my own living room.

[story 2]

"Morning, Clara," Mr. Henderson called out, his voice booming over the suburban quiet, punctuated by the whine of a power saw. He was currently attempting to hoist a stack of old siding onto a ladder that seemed determined to slip on the damp lawn. I nearly dropped my watering can. I managed a strained, "Morning, George," my voice coming out higher pitched than intended. I quickly turned away, focusing intently on straightening a misplaced throw pillow on the sofa, my fingers suddenly feeling clumsy and oversized. I could feel a heat rising up my neck, a prickling sensation across my skin that made me want to physically melt into the upholstery. It felt like everyone on the block was watching this spectacle of domestic upheaval, and I desperately wished the pavement would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I spent most of my afternoons trying to conduct my life in a state of near-silence. When Mr. Henderson was out—and he was out almost constantly now, armed with tools and an almost reckless enthusiasm—I’d try to stay inside, pretending the sounds of hammering and scraping were merely distant background noise. Today, he was installing some sort of elaborate wooden trellis near the fence. I walked past the shared boundary, my steps deliberately slow, trying to keep my gaze fixed on the ground. My palms were slick with a sudden, cold sweat. I felt a distinct urge to check my phone constantly, a frantic need for any external distraction to break the intense, internal pressure building behind my eyes. I just wanted the sun to stop shining on this whole neighborhood.
